bull.b: Sustained high margins and ROE indicate structural operating efficiency and pricing power across midstream assets. Durable margin profile supports internal funding for maintenance and growth, underpins dividend capacity, and provides resilience versus volume or commodity swings over multiple years.

Secured multi-year contracts (e.g., Neo 12.5y, Atlas 13y) and multiple project commercializations create long-term revenue visibility. Contract tenor and backlog de-risk volumes, bolster predictable fee revenue, and provide a multi-year runway for EBITDA growth independent of short-term commodity moves.

bull.t: Improved Liquidity Via Expanded Credit Facilities
bull.b: Larger committed facilities materially increase near-term liquidity and flexibility to fund capex, working capital, or bolt-on M&A while management finalizes longer-term financing. This reduces short-term refinancing risk and supports multi-year project execution plans.

bear.b: Higher leverage narrows financial flexibility and increases refinancing and interest-rate sensitivity through the capex build cycle. Prolonged elevated leverage could constrain capital allocation, raise covenant or rating risk, and limit ability to fund opportunistic investments for several years.

bear.t: Weak Free Cash Flow / Cash Conversion
bear.b: Low FCF relative to earnings implies limited discretionary cash after capex, reducing capacity to rapidly pay down debt, fund large growth projects or increase shareholder returns without external financing. Persistently weak cash conversion can prolong dependence on markets or asset recycling.

bear.t: High Growth CapEx And Execution/financing Dependency
bear.b: Large near-term capex requires external financing and successful partner or asset-sale arrangements. Delays in permits, legal challenges, or failure to secure favorable financing would raise project costs, postpone revenue realization, and amplify leverage and cash-flow pressure over the multi-year buildout.
